title: Ngā Mahi a Rongomaraeroa: The Art of Traditional Food Preservation | Te Reo Māori
subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: This lesson is grounded in the pūrākau of Rongomaraeroa, the atua associated with preserved foods, and connects specifically to the traditional pātaka (food storage pits) sites still visible along the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students will explore how Ngāti Kahungunu ancestors used seasonal abundance from the river and surrounding forests, preserving kai through methods like hāngī, fermentation, and drying to ensure community wellbeing throughout the year.
learningObjectives: Identify and describe in te reo Māori at least three traditional Māori food preservation methods (e.g., kōpaki, kāinga, tūhua) and their purposes.

Construct simple sentences in te reo Māori using kupu such as 'hāngī', 'pātaka', 'mātauranga', and 'kaitiakitanga' to explain the connection between food preservation and environmental sustainability.
Create a digital story or short video in te reo Māori that recounts the pūrākau of Rongomaraeroa and relates it to the local history of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Collaborate in small groups to design a modern adaptation of a traditional preservation method, presenting the idea in te reo Māori to the class.

activities.title: He Kōrero Tawhito: The Story of Rongomaraeroa
activities.description: Students will listen to and participate in the telling of the pūrākau of Rongomaraeroa, the atua of preserved foods. Using a storyboard template, they will sequence key events in te reo Māori, identifying and learning kupu hou (new words) related to food and preservation. This will be followed by a wānanga (discussion) on why these stories and practices were vital for Ngāti Kahungunu survival.

activities.title: He Haerenga: A Visit to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
activities.description: A guided walk to the awa (river) with a local kaumātua (elder) to visit known historical sites of pātaka (food storage pits). Students will observe the landscape, learn why these locations were chosen (e.g., proximity to water, drainage, sunlight), and use te reo Māori to describe what they see and hear. They will sketch the sites and label features in te reo.

activities.title: Ngā Mahi a te Ringa: Hands-On Preservation
activities.description: In the school's māra kai (garden), students will harvest seasonal produce (e.g., kūmara, corn). Guided by a kaiawhina (expert helper), they will prepare a simple hāngī or learn to make kāinga (dried corn) using traditional methods. Throughout the process, they will use and respond to instructions given in te reo Māori.

activities.title: He Whakaaro Hou: Modern Adaptations
activities.description: Working in small groups, students will brainstorm how a traditional method (e.g., fermentation, drying) could be adapted for use in a modern urban setting. They will create a simple poster or digital slide with labels and short explanations in te reo Māori, presenting their 'innovation' to the class and explaining its benefits using learned vocabulary.

resources: Kaumātua Hone Smith (local Ngāti Kahungunu elder and historian)
The book 'Ngā Kai o te Whenua: Food from the Land' by Dr. Jessica Hutchings
Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: Site maps and archaeological reports from the Hastings District Council iwi portal
Digital resource: 'Te Reo o te Kai' vocabulary lists on Te Whanake (tewhanake.maori.nz)

tasks: Completion of a sequenced storyboard for the Rongomaraeroa pūrākau using te reo Māori captions.
Participation in a recorded, small-group conversation in te reo Māori discussing the importance of one preservation method.
Creation and presentation of a modern adaptation poster with at least five correctly used te reo Māori kupu.

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Mana Reo (Level 4: Use relevant pronunciation, stress, rhythm, and intonation; initiate and sustain short conversations)
Te Reo Māori: Language Knowledge - Mana Reo (Level 4: Recognise that te reo Māori is organised in particular ways)

Social Sciences: Identity, Culture, and Organisation - Mana Whenua (Level 4: Understand how the ways in which leadership of groups is acquired and exercised have consequences for communities and societies)
Technology: Technological Practice - Level 4: Brief development, planning for practice, outcome development and evaluation
